fragments.content: Bipolar Disorder is a mood disorder characterized by recurrent episodes of mania/hypomania and depression. Bipolar I requires at least one manic episode (elevated/expansive/irritable mood with increased energy/activity lasting ≥1 week, causing marked impairment or requiring hospitalization). Bipolar II involves hypomanic episodes (similar symptoms but lasting ≥4 days without marked impairment) and major depressive episodes. Mania includes symptoms like grandiosity, decreased need for sleep, pressured speech, racing thoughts, distractibility, increased goal-directed activity, and risky behavior. Pathophysiology involves genetic factors (heritability ~70-80%), neurotransmitter dysregulation, circadian rhythm disruption, and altered neural circuitry in emotion regulation networks.

Acute mania treatment: lithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotics (e.g., olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapine, aripiprazole); severe cases may require combination therapy. Acute bipolar depression: quetiapine, lurasidone, lamotrigine, or lithium; antidepressants used cautiously due to mania risk. Maintenance: lithium remains first-line with strongest evidence for suicide prevention; alternatives include valproate, lamotrigine (especially for depression prevention), and atypical antipsychotics. Psychoeducation, CBT, interpersonal and social rhythm therapy (IPSRT), and family-focused therapy improve outcomes. Regular monitoring of medication levels, renal/thyroid function (lithium), liver function (valproate), and metabolic parameters is essential.

DSM-5-TR criteria: Manic episode requires abnormally elevated/expansive/irritable mood and increased energy/activity for ≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalized), plus ≥3 symptoms (grandiosity, decreased sleep need, pressured speech, flight of ideas, distractibility, increased activity, excessive risky activities) causing marked impairment. Hypomanic episode: similar symptoms for ≥4 consecutive days with unequivocal change in functioning but not markedly impairing. Bipolar I: ≥1 manic episode (depressive episode common but not required). Bipolar II: ≥1 hypomanic AND ≥1 major depressive episode, no history of mania. Cyclothymic disorder: ≥2 years of hypomanic and depressive symptoms not meeting full episode criteria. Specifiers include current episode type, severity, psychotic features, and rapid cycling (≥4 episodes/year).
